title: Security and Access Control with Views

description.task: Create views that restrict sensitive column access — demonstrating how views serve as a security layer.
description.context: Views can hide sensitive columns (like salary, SSN, password hash) from users who should only see a subset of the data. You GRANT SELECT on the view instead of the base table.
instructions: Run all CREATE TABLE, INSERT, CREATE VIEW statements.
Query public_users — confirm password_hash and salary are NOT visible.
Query hr_users — confirm salary is visible but password_hash is NOT.
Add a comment: why is it safer to query through views than granting direct table access?
Add a comment: can you update data through public_users view? Does the base table change?
description.hint: Views act as a permission boundary. Even if a user has SELECT on the view, they cannot access columns the view doesn't expose — unless they also have direct table access.
description.expectedOutput:
public_users: 5 columns, no password or salary
hr_users: includes salary, no password_hash

description.task: Replace a complex multi-join query with a view to simplify application code and reduce query duplication.
instructions: Run all CREATE TABLE, INSERT, CREATE VIEW statements.
First query: 4 orders ordered by total, largest first.
Second query: Alice and Carol (USA), Bob (UK) lifetime values.
Third query: Alice's 2 orders + Carol's 1 order.
Add a comment: how many duplicate JOIN queries does this view eliminate?
description.hint: Without the view, every application query that needs customer+order+items data must repeat the 3-table JOIN. The view defines it once — queries become simple SELECTs.
description.expectedOutput:
4 orders: Alice order1 ~1039.97, Carol ~139.96, Bob ~599.98, Alice order2 ~59.99
Lifetime: Alice~1099.96, Bob~599.98, Carol~139.96

description.task: Modify an existing view using CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW, then drop views cleanly.
instructions: Run CREATE TABLE, INSERT, initial CREATE VIEW, first SELECT.
Run CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW with the discounted_price column.
Run SELECT again — view now has 5 columns.
Run the pg_views query to confirm the view is gone.
Add a comment: what is the difference between DROP VIEW and DROP VIEW IF EXISTS?
description.hint: CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW updates the view definition without needing to DROP it first. If dependent objects (other views) reference it, DROP VIEW will fail unless CASCADE is used.
description.expectedOutput:
Initial view: 4 columns
Replaced view: 5 columns with discounted_price
After DROP: 0 rows in pg_views for active_products
